text.title: Predictive Analytics in PM — Agentic Workflow and Subagents
text.summary: How to structure the ML analytics pipeline as an agentic system, including subagent roles, prompt patterns, and failure modes specific to LLM-driven ML orchestration.

section.title: Pipeline Architecture

rule.statement: Treat the analytics layer as a separate ML pipeline (analytics/ repo) feeding the PM tool through alerts and dashboards — never as a Slackbot.
rule.rationale: Slackbot coupling creates brittle real-time dependencies; a pipeline with explicit data contracts and scheduled runs is testable and auditable.

section.p: A subagent owns the data contract (issue → feature row), trains and refreshes models, monitors drift, and writes predictions to a predictions/ datastore ingested by the PM tool via webhook.

section.title: Recommended Subagents

list.item: data-contract-agent (sonnet): inspects PM tool exports, defines stable feature columns, flags inconsistent statuses or missing fields.
feature-engineering-agent (sonnet): proposes engineered features (planned vs. actual ratios, queue ages, owner-change counts, comment cadence) with rationale.
model-training-agent (sonnet): wraps scikit-learn / XGBoost / LightGBM / Prophet pipelines; outputs model + metrics + SHAP explanations.
drift-monitor-agent (haiku): nightly — compares incoming feature distributions vs. training set; raises alerts when KS/PSI exceeds threshold.
prediction-explainer-agent (opus): turns SHAP outputs into stakeholder-readable risk-register entries.
password-scrubber-agent: scrubs PM exports before any third-party model call; Jira/Linear data leaks vendor names, prices, customer info.

section.reference: Baseline GBR regressor — beat this before claiming ML adds value
Feature contract schema emitted by the data-contract agent

example.note: Data-contract agent prompt — forces strict YAML output, explicit leakage exclusions:
example.code:
You are the data-contract agent. Inputs: 1. Sample of 5,000 issue rows from the source PM tool (CSV). 2. Project metadata (type, sponsor, budget, dates).
Emit STRICT YAML: features: - name: <snake_case> type: numeric|categorical|boolean|datetime source_field: <jira_field_id> nullable: true|false notes: "..." target: name: schedule_slip_days|budget_overrun_pct|risk_materialized type: regression|classification rationale: "..." exclusions: [ ... fields with PII/credentials ... ] data_quality_rules: - "status not in {Open, In Progress, Done} → flag" - "actual_start > actual_end → drop row"

example.note: Explanation prompt for SHAP → risk-register entry:
example.code: Given SHAP values for prediction id=X (top 5 features by |contribution|), produce a 3-sentence English explanation suitable for a risk-register entry. Cite feature name, direction, and magnitude. Do not editorialize beyond data.

antipattern.description: LLM "predicts" budget overruns from prose narrative without running a model.
antipattern.reason: LLMs are not numeric forecasters. They confabulate plausible-sounding numbers. Train a model; LLMs draft, explain, and orchestrate.

antipattern.description: Random train/test split used for project data.
antipattern.reason: Random splits leak future project data into past training. Group splits by project_id or program are mandatory.

antipattern.description: Agent declares "deployed" after model.fit() without wiring a drift monitor.
antipattern.reason: Shipping a model without monitoring is silent failure. Force a monitoring task into the SDD chain before calling any deployment complete.

antipattern.description: Sending raw PM CSV rows to a third-party LLM API.
antipattern.reason: PM data contains salary, vendor pricing, customer names. Tokenize or run on-prem; never send raw rows externally.
